story:
The train pulled in at 6:00 p.m. Jason hurried to Ellie, and they sat on her porch swing, holding hands. He broke the news: the Army was sending him to Germany for twenty months. Ellie stiffened, tears in her eyes. He reminded her he had six days’ leave, and they spent every minute of it together. He left, promising the months would fly.

They did. Jason returned, and for two months they spent every weekend and most nights together. Ellie was happy. Then September came. Jason started college and worked two jobs. At first he visited two or three times a week; soon it was two or three times a month. By the end of his sophomore year, his visits were rare. During his junior year he became engaged and married. He never saw or spoke to Ellie again. She learned of his marriage third-hand, mourned for months, then moved away to make a new life.

Jason and his wife settled down after college, and for thirty years he believed himself happily married. One ordinary evening, he came home expecting his usual welcome. The house was silent. In the kitchen, he found a letter addressed to him. Without opening it, he whispered, “She left me for another man.” “Oh, God. Oh, God, no,” he murmured as he read: she had left him for another man. “Gone? After all these years? What’s going to happen to me?” For weeks he searched for answers and found none.

One Sunday morning, the phone rang. “Hello? Yes, this is Jason. Who is this? Huh? Ellie? Ellie who?” Then he came alive. “My Ellie? Is it really you?” She was a widow and moving back. He asked her to dinner to tell her everything.

At the restaurant he feared she wouldn’t come, but then she called his name. They smiled and hugged. “Ellie, I would have known you anywhere. You haven’t changed a bit.” They sat and looked into each other’s eyes, as if searching. Jason saw what he wanted to see: an answer.

She asked what had happened. “I don’t know. She just left me…for another man,” he said. He spoke of lonely nights, of thinking of Ellie to get through them. “I thought I had the perfect marriage, and then it was gone.”

Ellie sighed. “Losing someone you love is painful, isn’t it, Jason?” He agreed, then drifted into memories. “Those were the days,” he said softly. He asked about her life. She told him she had moved to Texas, married Dean, started a small business, had a daughter. She showed him a photo. He glanced, then pulled out his own, talked of his daughters and first grandchild, and shook his head at the memory of the letter. “I loved her, but she didn’t love me, and I don’t know why.”

“I can understand it,” Ellie said. “People pretend. People use people.” She paused. “I knew someone who loved a man who didn’t love her back. He used her and then he left her.” Jason missed the point. He circled again to his pain. She told him Dean had died a year after their daughter was born. He offered sympathy, then returned to himself. At last he took her hands. “Ellie, I should have contacted you right after my wife left me. I shou—”

Ellie pulled away and cried. “Suddenly, after all these years, I’m important. Why did you let us disappear, Jason? Why didn’t you want me back then? Why didn’t you see I could have made you happy?”

He faltered. “Life was good until my wife left me. Then I was so unhappy. And then…I thought of you. But now we’re together. The past is gone. We have the present and the future.”

Ellie stood. “Yes, the past is gone—and what we had is gone, too. What you and I hoped for this evening didn’t happen. I’m sorry…for both of us. Good-bye, Jason.”

He reached for her. “Ellie…please, don’t leave me.” After she left, he reread his wife’s letter, dropped it, covered his face, and sobbed. “What’s going to become of me?”
